name: Xi Wangmu
subtitle: Queen Mother of the West
description:
Xi Wangmu, the Queen Mother of the West, stands as one of the most ancient and revered deities in the Chinese pantheon. Her mythology, spanning from the Shang dynasty oracle bones to contemporary religious practices, presents a complex and evolving figure. Initially depicted as a fearsome goddess of plague and punishment, she gradually transformed into the benevolent ruler of the immortals, residing in her paradisiacal realm on Mount Kunlun. Xi Wangmu embodies the ultimate yin principle of the cosmos, representing the feminine, the west, and the transformative power of death and rebirth. Her primary role is as the guardian of the Peaches of Immortality, which ripen only every few thousand years and grant varying degrees of longevity and even eternal life to those who consume them. She also possesses the elixir of immortality, further solidifying her status as a deity of longevity and transcendence. 

Her origins are shrouded in antiquity. The earliest textual references, found in the *Shanhaijing* (Classic of Mountains and Seas), portray her as a formidable figure. This early Xi Wangmu is described with a human face, a leopard's tail, and tiger's teeth, dwelling in a cave on Jade Mountain. She is associated with the control of plagues, punishments, and the five calamities, a far cry from the later benevolent image. According to the *Shanhaijing*, she is served by the mythical bird, the Peng, and the mythical beast, the 勝遇 (shèng yù). This primordial Xi Wangmu embodies raw, untamed power, a stark contrast to the refined queen of later traditions. 

The transformation of Xi Wangmu's image likely occurred over centuries, influenced by philosophical and religious developments, particularly the rise of Daoism. As Daoism emphasized the pursuit of immortality and harmony with nature, Xi Wangmu's role shifted towards a more positive and aspirational figure. She became associated with the attainment of transcendence and the secrets of eternal life, attracting the devotion of those seeking enlightenment and escape from mortality. Her residence on Mount Kunlun, a mythical mountain believed to be the axis mundi connecting earth and heaven, further enhanced her status as a cosmic deity. 

Mount Kunlun, in Xi Wangmu's mythology, is not merely a geographical location but a symbolic representation of paradise and the gateway to immortality. It is described as a place of unparalleled beauty, adorned with jade palaces, crystal halls, and rivers of immortality. Her Peach Orchard, a central feature of Kunlun, contains peaches of varying maturity, each granting a different level of longevity and immortality. The first type ripens every 3,000 years and grants an extended lifespan; the second type ripens every 6,000 years and grants immortality and the ability to fly; the third type ripens every 9,000 years and grants eternal life equal to that of heaven and earth. The consumption of these peaches is a privilege reserved for the gods and immortals, highlighting Xi Wangmu's control over the ultimate reward of Daoist cultivation. 

One of the most famous stories involving Xi Wangmu is her meeting with King Mu of Zhou (c. 10th century BCE), documented in the *Mu Tianzi Zhuan* (The Tale of King Mu). This account describes King Mu's journey westward, guided by his eight magical horses, until he reached Mount Kunlun and was received by Xi Wangmu. They exchanged poems and gifts, and she hosted a banquet by the Jade Pool. This encounter symbolizes the connection between the mortal world and the realm of the immortals, with Xi Wangmu acting as the intermediary. It also highlights the allure of immortality and the pursuit of divine favor. 

Another significant myth associated with Xi Wangmu is the story of Chang'e and Houyi. According to legend, Xi Wangmu gifted the elixir of immortality to the hero Houyi as a reward for his heroic act of shooting down nine of the ten suns that were scorching the earth. However, his wife Chang'e consumed the elixir herself and floated to the moon, where she became the moon goddess. This story explores themes of ambition, sacrifice, and the consequences of seeking immortality. It also reinforces Xi Wangmu's role as the distributor of immortality and the arbiter of fate. 

Xi Wangmu's Peach Banquet (蟠桃会), held periodically when her peaches ripen, is a grand celestial event to which all the gods and immortals are invited. This banquet is a symbol of divine harmony and the celebration of eternal life. However, the story of Sun Wukong (the Monkey King) disrupting the Peach Banquet, as depicted in the *Journey to the West* (Xiyou Ji), introduces an element of chaos and rebellion into the otherwise orderly celestial realm. Sun Wukong, appointed as the guardian of the Peach Orchard, consumes all the ripened peaches, drinks the immortal wine, and steals Laozi's pills of immortality, committing a major crime against heaven and challenging the established order. This episode highlights the tension between the pursuit of immortality and the maintenance of cosmic balance. 

In some versions of the legend of the Cowherd and Weaver Girl (Zhinu), Xi Wangmu plays a role in separating the lovers. According to these accounts, she created the Milky Way with her jade hairpin to keep the Cowherd and Weaver Girl apart, only allowing them to meet once a year on the Qixi Festival (Double Seventh Festival). This role, though seemingly harsh, underscores her position as a guardian of celestial order and a figure who enforces the boundaries between the mortal and immortal realms. 

Xi Wangmu's symbolism is rich and multifaceted. The peaches of immortality represent longevity, vitality, and the attainment of transcendence. The phoenix, a mythical bird associated with immortality and rebirth, is one of her sacred animals. The jade palace and the Jade Pool symbolize the beauty and serenity of her paradisiacal realm. The three-legged crow, often depicted as her messenger, represents the connection between the heavens and the earth. The nine-tailed fox, in some traditions, serves as her guardian or attendant, symbolizing wisdom and cunning. The color gold, associated with divinity and royalty, is often used in depictions of Xi Wangmu. Cinnabar, a red mineral associated with alchemy and immortality, is also a significant symbol. 

The cultural significance of Xi Wangmu extends beyond religious belief. She is a symbol of feminine power, wisdom, and grace. Her worship is particularly popular among women, who seek her blessings for longevity, good health, and protection. Temples dedicated to her worship can be found throughout China and Taiwan, particularly in the Kunlun Mountains region, Tai Shan (Mount Tai), and Fujian Province. Her birthday, celebrated on the third day of the third lunar month, is an important festival marked by peach-shaped offerings and elaborate rituals. The Qixi Festival and the Mid-Autumn Festival are also connected to her through the legends of the Weaver Girl and Chang'e, respectively. 

In conclusion, Xi Wangmu is a complex and multifaceted deity whose mythology has evolved over millennia. From a fearsome goddess of plague and punishment to the benevolent Queen of Immortality, she represents the transformative power of the feminine principle and the enduring human quest for eternal life. Her stories, symbols, and cultural significance continue to resonate in contemporary Chinese society, making her one of the most important figures in the Chinese pantheon.
titles: Queen Mother of the West (西王母)
Golden Mother of the Jade Pool (瑶池金母)
Primordial Ruler of the Western Heaven
Mother Goddess of the West (西王母娘娘)

mythology_content.overview: Xi Wangmu is one of the oldest deities in Chinese religion, appearing in texts from the Shang dynasty oracle bones through to contemporary worship. Her mythology evolved dramatically over millennia, from a fearsome goddess of plague and punishment to the benevolent Queen of Immortality.

peachesOfImmortality.description: Xi Wangmu's most famous treasure is her garden of immortal peaches on Mount Kunlun.

threeTypes.effect: Grant eternal life equal to heaven and earth

peachesOfImmortality.peachBanquet: Every eon, Xi Wangmu hosts the Peach Banquet (蟠桃会), inviting all gods and immortals to feast on the ripened peaches. This is the most prestigious event in the celestial calendar.

mountKunlun.description: Xi Wangmu's realm is the legendary Mount Kunlun (昆仑山), described as the axis mundi connecting earth and heaven.
features: The cosmic mountain at the center/west of the world, beyond mortal reach
Jade Palace by the Jade Pool (瑶池)
Rivers of immortality, gardens of jade trees, halls of crystal

keyMyths.title: Ancient Origins
keyMyths.description: In the earliest texts (Shanhaijing/Classic of Mountains and Seas), Xi Wangmu appears as a fearsome goddess with a human face, leopard's tail, and tiger's teeth. She dwells in a cave on Jade Mountain, controlling plague, punishment, and the five calamities.

keyMyths.title: Meeting King Mu of Zhou
keyMyths.description: Emperor Mu of Zhou (c. 10th century BCE) journeyed west in his chariot drawn by eight magical horses. He reached Mount Kunlun and was received by Xi Wangmu. They exchanged poems and gifts, and she hosted a banquet by the Jade Pool.

keyMyths.title: Chang'e and the Moon
keyMyths.description: Xi Wangmu gave the elixir of immortality to the hero Houyi as a reward for shooting down nine of the ten suns. His wife Chang'e drank the elixir and floated to the moon, where she still dwells.

keyMyths.title: Journey to the West
keyMyths.description: Xi Wangmu's Peach Banquet is disrupted when Sun Wukong, appointed guardian of the orchard, eats all the ripened peaches, drinks the immortal wine, and consumes Laozi's pills of immortality.

keyMyths.title: The Weaver Girl (Zhinu)
keyMyths.description: In some versions of the Cowherd and Weaver Girl legend, Xi Wangmu separated the lovers by creating the Milky Way with her jade hairpin, allowing them to meet only once per year on Qixi.

yinPrinciple: Supreme embodiment of cosmic yin energy
Represents the west, autumn, metal element
Balances the yang of the Jade Emperor or Dong Wanggong
Controls death and rebirth, the cycle of transformation
motherOfImmortals: All female immortals are under her authority
Grants immortality through her peaches and elixirs
Represents the goal of Daoist cultivation

family.consort: Jade Emperor or Dong Wanggong (King Father of the East)

attendants.name: Three Blue Birds (三青鸟)
attendants.role: Messengers who bring her food and serve as heralds

attendants.name: Jade Maidens
attendants.role: Female immortals who attend her court

attendants.name: Nine-Tailed Fox
attendants.role: Guardian or attendant in some traditions

attendants.name: Phoenix and Peacocks
attendants.role: Sacred birds that grace her gardens

notableInteractions.name: King Mu of Zhou
notableInteractions.role: Legendary meeting and exchange of poetry

notableInteractions.name: Emperor Wu of Han
notableInteractions.role: Accounts of her appearing to grant him peaches

notableInteractions.name: Sun Wukong
notableInteractions.role: His theft from her orchard was a major crime against heaven

sacredSites.name: Mount Kunlun Region
sacredSites.description: Temples in the Kunlun Mountains area of western China

sacredSites.name: Tai Shan (Mount Tai)
sacredSites.description: Bixia Yuanjun temple, where she is honored as a mother goddess

sacredSites.name: Taiwan
sacredSites.description: Numerous 'Golden Mother' temples, particularly popular with female worshippers

sacredSites.name: Fujian Province
sacredSites.description: Major temple complexes dedicated to her worship

festivals.name: Xi Wangmu's Birthday (王母娘娘圣诞)
festivals.date: Third day of the third lunar month
festivals.description: Celebrated with peach-shaped offerings and elaborate rituals

festivals.name: Double Seven Festival (七夕)
festivals.description: Connected to her through the Weaver Girl story

festivals.name: Mid-Autumn Festival
festivals.description: Chang'e's story links this festival to Xi Wangmu's elixir

offerings: Peaches (real and symbolic peach-shaped buns, cakes, candies)
Sweet foods (cakes, candied fruits, sweet rice)
patronage: Those seeking longevity and good health

sources.title: Shanhaijing (山海经)
sources.author: Unknown
sources.text: Describes Xi Wangmu as a fearsome goddess with a human face, leopard's tail, and tiger's teeth, dwelling on Jade Mountain and controlling plagues and punishments.

sources.title: Mu Tianzi Zhuan (穆天子传)
sources.author: Unknown
sources.text: Records the legendary meeting between King Mu of Zhou and Xi Wangmu on Mount Kunlun, where they exchanged poems and gifts.

sources.title: Huainanzi (淮南子)
sources.author: Liu An
sources.text: Contains the story of Houyi and Chang'e, where Xi Wangmu gives Houyi the elixir of immortality, which Chang'e then consumes to fly to the moon.

sources.title: Journey to the West (西游记)
sources.author: Wu Cheng'en
sources.text: Features Xi Wangmu's Peach Banquet, which is disrupted by Sun Wukong's theft of the immortal peaches and wine.

sources.title: Liexian Zhuan (列仙传)
sources.author: Liu Xiang (traditionally attributed)
sources.text: Biographies of various immortals, including mentions of Xi Wangmu and her role in granting immortality.

sources.title: Taoist Scriptures
sources.author: Various
sources.text: Numerous Taoist texts discuss Xi Wangmu as a central figure in the pursuit of immortality and spiritual transcendence.

crossCulturalParallels.connection: Great goddess of magic and life
Keeper of the apples of immortality

archetype.description: Xi Wangmu embodies the Great Goddess archetype - primordial feminine power, keeper of life and death, guardian of immortality, dwelling in the sacred mountain paradise.

hoverInfo.quick: Immortal Queen of Mount Kunlun, Guardian of the Peaches of Immortality

listDisplay.secondary: Guardian of the Peaches of Immortality
listDisplay.expandedContent: Xi Wangmu is one of the oldest and most powerful goddesses in Chinese mythology, ruling from her paradise on Mount Kunlun as keeper of the elixir of eternal life.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Xi Wangmu is one of the oldest and most powerful goddesses in Chinese mythology, the immortal Queen who rules from her paradise on Mount Kunlun. Guardian of the Peaches of Immortality and keeper of the elixir of eternal life, she presides over female immortals and represents the ultimate yin principle of the cosmos.

epithets: Golden Mother of the Jade Pool
